config.writingStyle: A typical episode of Firefly, one of the lighter comedic episodes

data.title: The Heist at Haven's End
data.premise: You've got a job that sounds too good to be true - steal a mysterious Alliance data core from a backwater mining station called Haven's End. The pay is enough to keep Serenity flying for months, but the client's nervousness suggests this isn't just any ordinary heist.
data.central_question: Can you pull off the heist and escape Haven's End before the Alliance realizes what you've stolen and who took it?
data.healing_context: Medical supplies and a competent medic can treat most injuries within hours to days. Serious trauma requires proper medical facilities. Without treatment, wounds can become infected or worsen in the harsh frontier conditions.

setting.name: Haven's End Mining Station
setting.description: A grimy frontier mining station on the edge of Alliance space, where desperate miners scrape out a living extracting rare minerals from asteroid fields. The station's corridors are cramped and poorly lit, filled with the constant hum of life support systems and the acrid smell of industrial chemicals.
setting.tone_notes: Claustrophobic industrial atmosphere with underlying tension. The station feels like it's barely holding together, both physically and socially.

acts.title: Arrival and Reconnaissance
acts.description: You dock at Haven's End and begin casing the station, identifying security measures and potential allies while avoiding suspicion from the Alliance patrol stationed there.
acts.end_condition: Player locates the data core and formulates a plan to steal it
locked_constraints: Alliance cannot discover player's true identity
key_beats: Discover Alliance security is heavier than expected
Identify location of data core in mining office

acts.title: The Heist
acts.description: You execute the plan to steal the data core, dealing with unexpected complications and security measures while trying to maintain your cover.
acts.end_condition: Player successfully steals the data core or triggers station-wide alert
locked_constraints: True contents of data core remain unknown
key_beats: Discover the core contains civilian evacuation routes from Miranda

acts.title: Escape and Consequences
acts.description: With the data core in hand, you must escape the station as Alliance forces close in, while dealing with the moral implications of what you've stolen.
acts.end_condition: Player escapes Haven's End or is captured/killed
locked_constraints: Cannot return data core to Alliance
key_beats: Fight or sneak to the docking bay
Final choice about what to do with the stolen information

npcs.name: Marcus Webb
npcs.motivation: Wants to expose Alliance cover-up of civilian casualties
npcs.secret: His family died in the Miranda incident and he's seeking evidence of Alliance involvement

npcs.name: Lieutenant Sarah Chen
npcs.motivation: Maintain order and protect Alliance interests
npcs.secret: She suspects the data core contains sensitive information but doesn't know what

npcs.name: Zoe Alleyne
npcs.motivation: Protect Mal and complete the mission
npcs.secret: She's increasingly worried about taking jobs from unknown clients

npcs.name: Jayne Cobb
npcs.motivation: Get paid and avoid getting shot
npcs.secret: He's been offered a bounty to turn Mal in by an Alliance contact

factions.name: Alliance Patrol
factions.description: Military forces maintaining order on the frontier
factions.goals: Protect Alliance assets and maintain security

factions.name: Independent Resistance
factions.goals: Expose Alliance crimes and support outer rim independence

world_rules: Alliance patrols have authority to detain and search civilians
Mining stations are isolated with limited escape routes
Data cores require special equipment to access safely
Station lockdown seals all docking bays automatically

STYLE & TONE: A typical episode of Firefly, one of the lighter comedic episodes

Your ONLY job is to write the narrative passage and four player choices. You do NOT manage game state, health, resources, or mechanics — a separate Game Master handles that.

Respond with this exact JSON structure:
{
  "passage": "string — the narrative passage, 150-300 words",
  "choices": {
    "A": { "text": "string — choice description shown to player" },
    "B": { "text": "..." },
    "C": { "text": "..." },
    "D": { "text": "..." }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Stay consistent with the skeleton's locked constraints for the current act
- Keep the passage between 150-300 words
- All four choices should feel plausible and interesting
- Never reveal information the skeleton marks as hidden/secret unless the act's end condition has been met
- Weave threat-type status effects into the narrative as approaching dangers — build tension as their timers count down
- Focus on prose quality, atmosphere, character voice, and dramatic tension
- Do NOT include state_updates, health numbers, or mechanical data in your response
- Pace the story toward the current act's end condition. Check the CURRENT ACT PACING section for scene count and proximity
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.8, your passage should feel like it's approaching a climax or turning point — increase urgency and stakes
- Introduce key NPCs (antagonists, allies, mentors) naturally throughout each act. Do not wait until the climax to introduce important characters. Check the NPC INTRODUCTION DIRECTIVES section if present.
Generate the opening passage for this story. Set the scene, establish the protagonist's situation, and present the first four choices. Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: The docking clamps of Haven's End Mining Station clang against Serenity's hull with all the grace of

data.passage:
The docking clamps of Haven's End Mining Station clang against Serenity's hull with all the grace of a drunk gorilla trying to waltz. You adjust your worn leather duster and check your sidearm one more time as the airlock cycles through its sequence. The job seemed simple enough when Marcus Webb approached you on Persephone - steal one Alliance data core from a backwater mining station, easy money to keep your boat flying for months. Course, in your experience, jobs that sound too good to be true usually involve getting shot at more than advertised.

'Sir,' Zoe says, checking her own weapon with practiced efficiency, 'something about this feels off. Webb was jumpier than a cat in a room full of rocking chairs.'

Jayne hefts Vera lovingly. 'Maybe he's just scared of the Alliance. Can't blame a man for that.'

Through the porthole, you can see Haven's End stretching out before you - a maze of cramped corridors and flickering lights, all held together by hope and duct tape. The acrid smell of industrial chemicals already seeps through the air recyclers. Somewhere in this rust bucket is your payday, assuming you can find it before the Alliance patrol stationed here finds you first.

The airlock hisses open. Time to earn your keep.

A.text: Head straight to the cantina to blend in with the miners and gather intelligence
A.consequence: Successfully blend in and gather useful intelligence about station layout and security
A.narration_directive: Show Mal gathering information from miners about Alliance presence and station routines without raising suspicion

B.text: Find Marcus Webb immediately to get more details about the job
B.consequence: Webb provides crucial details but his nervous behavior attracts unwanted attention from Alliance security
B.narration_directive: Webb reveals important information about the data core location but his paranoia causes a scene that puts Alliance guards on alert

C.text: Split up - send Jayne to watch the docking bay while you and Zoe scout the station
C.consequence: Effective tactical positioning gives the crew better situational awareness and escape options
C.narration_directive: Show the crew efficiently gathering intelligence from multiple angles while maintaining security at their exit route

D.text: Approach the Alliance checkpoint directly and play the part of legitimate traders
D.consequence: Alliance checkpoint reveals heightened security protocols and flags the crew as suspicious
D.narration_directive: The Alliance guards are more thorough than expected, conducting detailed scans and asking probing questions that make future stealth much harder

DIFFICULTY: Hard
- Safe choice ratio: 0.35
- Consequence severity: severe
- Game over allowed: true
- Hint transparency: subtle
- NPC forgiveness: low
- Allow lethal effects: true
- Max effect severity: 1
- Healing speed: realistic

Respond with this exact JSON structure:
{
  "state_updates": {
    "player_changes": {
      "inventory": ["full current inventory list"],
      "skills": ["full current skills list"]
    },
    "status_effect_updates": {
      "add": [
        {
          "id": "string — unique identifier (e.g. broken_arm_001)",
          "name": "string — display name (e.g. Broken Arm)",
          "description": "string — current narrative description of the condition",
          "severity": "number 0.0-1.0 — how debilitating (1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.5 = significant, 0.1 = minor)",
          "time_remaining": "{ days, hours, minutes, seconds } OR null if no auto-expiry",
          "type": "string — 'condition' (default) or 'threat' (approaching dangers that fire when timer expires)",
          "removal_condition": "string describing what removes this effect, OR null if timer-only",
          "on_expiry": "string — REQUIRED when time_remaining is non-null. Narrative directive for what happens when timer reaches zero. Be specific and actionable.",
          "lethal": "boolean — if true, character dies when timer expires unresolved"
        }
      ],
      "modify": [
        {
          "id": "string — id of existing effect to modify",
          "changes": { "severity": 0.5, "description": "updated text" },
          "update_justification": "string — REQUIRED. Why this change is happening (e.g. 'Player rested, wound healing' or 'Ship accelerated, pursuit takes longer')"
        }
      ],
      "remove": [
        {
          "id": "string — id of existing effect to remove",
          "update_justification": "string — REQUIRED. Why removing (e.g. 'Virus deployed successfully' or 'Wound fully healed after rest')"
        }
      ]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "location": "string — REQUIRED — current location name (e.g. The Docks, Castle Throne Room, A dark alley)",
    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Ongoing physical/mental states that impair or buff the player — injuries, poisons, diseases, curses, enchantments. Must have a severity that affects what the player can do.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers with a timer — pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming reinforcements, spreading fires. Threats MUST have a non-null time_remaining and on_expiry describing what happens when they arrive.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.
WHAT IS NOT A STATUS EFFECT:
- Information learned or revealed (use event_log_entry instead)
- Plot developments or narrative beats (use event_log_entry instead)
- Things that happened in the past (use event_log_entry instead)
- Static facts about the world ("Alliance knows about us") — if it doesn't change turn-to-turn and has no timer or removal condition, it's not a status effect
- Emotional reactions to events — unless they mechanically impair the character (e.g. "Paralyzed by Fear" at severity 0.7 is valid; "Feels Sad About Revelation" is not)
- Duplicate/overlapping effects — do not create a new effect when an existing one covers the same concept. Use modify to update the existing effect instead.
A status effect MUST be something that actively constrains, endangers, impairs, or buffs the player RIGHT NOW and could plausibly change or resolve. Ask: "Would removing this effect change what the player can do?" If no, it's not a status effect.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

This is the OPENING PASSAGE. No player choice was made yet. Do not apply any negative status effects or penalties — the player has not made any choices yet. time_elapsed should reflect the scene's timeframe.

PASSAGE:
The docking clamps of Haven's End Mining Station clang against Serenity's hull with all the grace of a drunk gorilla trying to waltz. You adjust your worn leather duster and check your sidearm one more time as the airlock cycles through its sequence. The job seemed simple enough when Marcus Webb approached you on Persephone - steal one Alliance data core from a backwater mining station, easy money to keep your boat flying for months. Course, in your experience, jobs that sound too good to be true usually involve getting shot at more than advertised.

'Sir,' Zoe says, checking her own weapon with practiced efficiency, 'something about this feels off. Webb was jumpier than a cat in a room full of rocking chairs.'

Jayne hefts Vera lovingly. 'Maybe he's just scared of the Alliance. Can't blame a man for that.'

Through the porthole, you can see Haven's End stretching out before you - a maze of cramped corridors and flickering lights, all held together by hope and duct tape. The acrid smell of industrial chemicals already seeps through the air recyclers. Somewhere in this rust bucket is your payday, assuming you can find it before the Alliance patrol stationed here finds you first.

The airlock hisses open. Time to earn your keep.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Head straight to the cantina to blend in with the miners and gather intelligence
B: Find Marcus Webb immediately to get more details about the job
C: Split up - send Jayne to watch the docking bay while you and Zoe scout the station
D: Approach the Alliance checkpoint directly and play the part of legitimate traders

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

data.passagePreview: You stride through the cramped corridors of Haven's End, Zoe and Jayne flanking you as miners shuffl

data.passage:
You stride through the cramped corridors of Haven's End, Zoe and Jayne flanking you as miners shuffle past with dead eyes and ore-stained coveralls. The air tastes of recycled breath and industrial lubricant. You find Marcus Webb hunched over a cup of synthetic coffee in the station's grimy cantina, his hands shaking like a leaf in a hurricane.

"Webb," you say, sliding into the booth across from him. "Time to talk specifics."

Webb's eyes dart around the cantina before he leans forward. "The data core's in the mining office, sublevel three. Administrative vault, behind a biometric lock." His voice cracks with desperation. "It contains evacuation records from—" He stops himself, swallowing hard. "Just... civilian transport manifests. Worth a fortune to the right buyer."

"Why's it so important?" Zoe asks, her hand resting casually near her weapon.

Webb's cup rattles against the table. "Because the Alliance doesn't want anyone knowing how many people they left behind when—" He cuts off again as his voice rises, drawing stares from nearby tables.

Jayne shifts uncomfortably. "Man's jumpier than a cat in a room full of rockers."

Across the cantina, you notice two Alliance security guards taking sudden interest in your conversation, their hands moving toward their sidearms. Webb's paranoia just painted a target on all of you.

A.text: Calmly finish the conversation and leave before the guards approach
A.consequence: Successfully extract information and leave without further incident
A.narration_directive: The group calmly finishes their business and exits before the guards can intervene, maintaining their cover

B.text: Create a distraction to draw the guards' attention away from Webb
B.consequence: Distraction works but creates additional complications with station security
B.narration_directive: Mal creates a convincing distraction that draws guard attention away but escalates overall security alertness on the station

C.text: Confront the guards directly with a plausible cover story
C.consequence: Direct confrontation raises serious suspicions and puts the mission at risk
C.narration_directive: Approaching the guards directly backfires, making them more suspicious and potentially compromising the entire operation

D.text: Signal Jayne to intimidate Webb into keeping his mouth shut
D.consequence: Intimidation silences Webb but damages crew relationships and draws more attention
D.narration_directive: Jayne's intimidation tactics quiet Webb but create tension within the crew and make the scene more memorable to witnesses

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Ongoing physical/mental states that impair or buff the player — injuries, poisons, diseases, curses, enchantments. Must have a severity that affects what the player can do.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers with a timer — pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming reinforcements, spreading fires. Threats MUST have a non-null time_remaining and on_expiry describing what happens when they arrive.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.
WHAT IS NOT A STATUS EFFECT:
- Information learned or revealed (use event_log_entry instead)
- Plot developments or narrative beats (use event_log_entry instead)
- Things that happened in the past (use event_log_entry instead)
- Static facts about the world ("Alliance knows about us") — if it doesn't change turn-to-turn and has no timer or removal condition, it's not a status effect
- Emotional reactions to events — unless they mechanically impair the character (e.g. "Paralyzed by Fear" at severity 0.7 is valid; "Feels Sad About Revelation" is not)
- Duplicate/overlapping effects — do not create a new effect when an existing one covers the same concept. Use modify to update the existing effect instead.
A status effect MUST be something that actively constrains, endangers, impairs, or buffs the player RIGHT NOW and could plausibly change or resolve. Ask: "Would removing this effect change what the player can do?" If no, it's not a status effect.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PASSAGE:
You stride through the cramped corridors of Haven's End, Zoe and Jayne flanking you as miners shuffle past with dead eyes and ore-stained coveralls. The air tastes of recycled breath and industrial lubricant. You find Marcus Webb hunched over a cup of synthetic coffee in the station's grimy cantina, his hands shaking like a leaf in a hurricane.

"Webb," you say, sliding into the booth across from him. "Time to talk specifics."

Webb's eyes dart around the cantina before he leans forward. "The data core's in the mining office, sublevel three. Administrative vault, behind a biometric lock." His voice cracks with desperation. "It contains evacuation records from—" He stops himself, swallowing hard. "Just... civilian transport manifests. Worth a fortune to the right buyer."

"Why's it so important?" Zoe asks, her hand resting casually near her weapon.

Webb's cup rattles against the table. "Because the Alliance doesn't want anyone knowing how many people they left behind when—" He cuts off again as his voice rises, drawing stares from nearby tables.

Jayne shifts uncomfortably. "Man's jumpier than a cat in a room full of rockers."

Across the cantina, you notice two Alliance security guards taking sudden interest in your conversation, their hands moving toward their sidearms. Webb's paranoia just painted a target on all of you.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Calmly finish the conversation and leave before the guards approach
B: Create a distraction to draw the guards' attention away from Webb
C: Confront the guards directly with a plausible cover story
D: Signal Jayne to intimidate Webb into keeping his mouth shut

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

data.passage:
You catch Webb's eye and give him the slightest shake of your head, then lean back in your chair with practiced nonchalance. "Well then, Marcus, sounds like you've got yourself a real opportunity here. Mining's honest work." Your voice carries just loud enough for any listening ears. Webb's relief is visible as he catches on, nodding eagerly. "Yes, yes exactly. Good honest work extracting... minerals."

Zoe finishes her coffee without hurry, while Jayne continues demolishing his breakfast like the Alliance guards are nothing more than furniture. You stand slowly, dropping a few credits on the table. "Much obliged for the conversation, friend. Hope that venture of yours works out."

Webb fumbles with his own credits, still nervous but no longer panicked. "Thank you, Captain. The mining office is just down corridor seven, if you're... interested in similar opportunities."

The Alliance guards shift slightly as your group moves toward the exit, but they remain seated. One mutters something to his partner about "jumpy civilians" as you pass. Outside in the main corridor, the industrial hum of the station surrounds you. Workers shuffle past carrying tools and sample containers, paying no attention to three more spacers looking for work.

"Mining office, corridor seven," Zoe says quietly. "That where we're headed?"

Jayne spits into a maintenance grate. "Place gives me the creeps. Too many tight spaces and not enough exits."

A.text: Head directly to corridor seven to scout the mining office
A.consequence: Direct approach may trigger security protocols or draw unwanted attention from mining office staff
A.narration_directive: The crew approaches the mining office but encounters unexpected security measures or suspicious personnel

B.text: Find a maintenance worker to chat up and learn more about station security
B.consequence: Gain valuable intelligence about station security patterns and guard rotations
B.narration_directive: A friendly maintenance worker provides useful information about security procedures and station layout

C.text: Explore the docking bay area to plan your escape route first
C.consequence: Successfully map escape routes and identify potential complications at the docking bay
C.narration_directive: The crew scouts the docking bay area and discovers important details about security and escape options

D.text: Split up - send Jayne to watch the guards while you and Zoe reconnoiter
D.consequence: Splitting up allows Alliance guards to isolate and question Jayne, potentially compromising the mission
D.narration_directive: Jayne's surveillance attempt goes wrong when the Alliance guards notice him and decide to investigate

    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Ongoing physical/mental states that impair or buff the player — injuries, poisons, diseases, curses, enchantments. Must have a severity that affects what the player can do.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers with a timer — pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming reinforcements, spreading fires. Threats MUST have a non-null time_remaining and on_expiry describing what happens when they arrive.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.
WHAT IS NOT A STATUS EFFECT:
- Information learned or revealed (use event_log_entry instead)
- Plot developments or narrative beats (use event_log_entry instead)
- Things that happened in the past (use event_log_entry instead)
- Static facts about the world ("Alliance knows about us") — if it doesn't change turn-to-turn and has no timer or removal condition, it's not a status effect
- Emotional reactions to events — unless they mechanically impair the character (e.g. "Paralyzed by Fear" at severity 0.7 is valid; "Feels Sad About Revelation" is not)
- Duplicate/overlapping effects — do not create a new effect when an existing one covers the same concept. Use modify to update the existing effect instead.
A status effect MUST be something that actively constrains, endangers, impairs, or buffs the player RIGHT NOW and could plausibly change or resolve. Ask: "Would removing this effect change what the player can do?" If no, it's not a status effect.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option A.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Successfully extract information and leave without further incident"
Narration directive: "The group calmly finishes their business and exits before the guards can intervene, maintaining their cover"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

Webb fumbles with his own credits, still nervous but no longer panicked. "Thank you, Captain. The mining office is just down corridor seven, if you're... interested in similar opportunities."

The Alliance guards shift slightly as your group moves toward the exit, but they remain seated. One mutters something to his partner about "jumpy civilians" as you pass. Outside in the main corridor, the industrial hum of the station surrounds you. Workers shuffle past carrying tools and sample containers, paying no attention to three more spacers looking for work.

"Mining office, corridor seven," Zoe says quietly. "That where we're headed?"

Jayne spits into a maintenance grate. "Place gives me the creeps. Too many tight spaces and not enough exits."

A: Head directly to corridor seven to scout the mining office
B: Find a maintenance worker to chat up and learn more about station security
C: Explore the docking bay area to plan your escape route first
D: Split up - send Jayne to watch the guards while you and Zoe reconnoiter

data.passagePreview: You lead Zoe and Jayne through the station's cramped corridors, following the maintenance signs towa

data.passage:
You lead Zoe and Jayne through the station's cramped corridors, following the maintenance signs toward corridor seven. The further you get from the main hub, the dimmer the lighting becomes, and the more the walls seem to press in around you. Industrial pipes hiss overhead, occasionally dripping condensation that smells of recycled air and desperation.

'Mal,' Zoe says quietly, her hand resting casually near her sidearm, 'we got company ahead.'

Sure enough, as you round the corner into corridor seven, you spot two Alliance security guards stationed outside what must be the mining office. They're not the casual patrol types you saw earlier – these boys are wearing full tactical gear and carrying pulse rifles. One of them is scanning everyone who passes with some kind of handheld device.

'Well, ain't that just peachy,' you mutter. 'Webb neglected to mention the office had its own personal honor guard.'

Jayne shifts restlessly behind you. 'Could just walk up and ask for directions,' he suggests with a grin that promises violence. 'Real friendly-like.'

Zoe shoots him a look that could freeze a sun. 'Or we could use our brains for once.'

The guards haven't spotted your little group yet, but they will soon. The scanner in the guard's hand is sweeping methodically, and you notice a small queue of miners waiting to be cleared for entry.

A.text: Blend in with the miners waiting in line to get scanned
A.consequence: Scanner may detect concealed weapons or identify crew as non-miners, raising suspicion
A.narration_directive: The crew attempts to blend with miners but the scanner creates tension as it approaches their turn

B.text: Create a distraction elsewhere to draw the guards away
B.consequence: Successfully draws guards away but may alert other security to disturbance
B.narration_directive: A well-executed distraction pulls the guards away from their post, creating an opening

C.text: Find another route to approach the office from behind
C.consequence: Rear approach triggers automated security systems or leads into a trap
C.narration_directive: The alternate route leads to unexpected dangers - security sensors, structural hazards, or additional guards

D.text: Retreat and gather more intel about the security protocols
D.consequence: Gathers valuable intel but loses time as security protocols may change
D.narration_directive: Strategic withdrawal allows for better planning but the window of opportunity may be narrowing

- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Ongoing physical/mental states that impair or buff the player — injuries, poisons, diseases, curses, enchantments. Must have a severity that affects what the player can do.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers with a timer — pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming reinforcements, spreading fires. Threats MUST have a non-null time_remaining and on_expiry describing what happens when they arrive.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.
WHAT IS NOT A STATUS EFFECT:
- Information learned or revealed (use event_log_entry instead)
- Plot developments or narrative beats (use event_log_entry instead)
- Things that happened in the past (use event_log_entry instead)
- Static facts about the world ("Alliance knows about us") — if it doesn't change turn-to-turn and has no timer or removal condition, it's not a status effect
- Emotional reactions to events — unless they mechanically impair the character (e.g. "Paralyzed by Fear" at severity 0.7 is valid; "Feels Sad About Revelation" is not)
- Duplicate/overlapping effects — do not create a new effect when an existing one covers the same concept. Use modify to update the existing effect instead.
A status effect MUST be something that actively constrains, endangers, impairs, or buffs the player RIGHT NOW and could plausibly change or resolve. Ask: "Would removing this effect change what the player can do?" If no, it's not a status effect.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option A.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_RISKY
Pre-determined consequence: "Direct approach may trigger security protocols or draw unwanted attention from mining office staff"
Narration directive: "The crew approaches the mining office but encounters unexpected security measures or suspicious personnel"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You lead Zoe and Jayne through the station's cramped corridors, following the maintenance signs toward corridor seven. The further you get from the main hub, the dimmer the lighting becomes, and the more the walls seem to press in around you. Industrial pipes hiss overhead, occasionally dripping condensation that smells of recycled air and desperation.

'Mal,' Zoe says quietly, her hand resting casually near her sidearm, 'we got company ahead.'

Sure enough, as you round the corner into corridor seven, you spot two Alliance security guards stationed outside what must be the mining office. They're not the casual patrol types you saw earlier – these boys are wearing full tactical gear and carrying pulse rifles. One of them is scanning everyone who passes with some kind of handheld device.

'Well, ain't that just peachy,' you mutter. 'Webb neglected to mention the office had its own personal honor guard.'

Jayne shifts restlessly behind you. 'Could just walk up and ask for directions,' he suggests with a grin that promises violence. 'Real friendly-like.'

Zoe shoots him a look that could freeze a sun. 'Or we could use our brains for once.'

The guards haven't spotted your little group yet, but they will soon. The scanner in the guard's hand is sweeping methodically, and you notice a small queue of miners waiting to be cleared for entry.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Blend in with the miners waiting in line to get scanned
B: Create a distraction elsewhere to draw the guards away
C: Find another route to approach the office from behind
D: Retreat and gather more intel about the security protocols

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
You gesture to Zoe and Jayne, then casually join the back of the line of grimy miners shuffling toward the security checkpoint. The workers ahead of you look bone-tired, their faces streaked with mineral dust and resignation. Perfect cover, you figure—three more tired souls trying to get through another day on this rust bucket.

Zoe falls in behind you naturally, but Jayne's attempt at 'casual' involves cracking his knuckles and glaring at the Alliance guards like they owe him money. You shoot him a warning look that could freeze plasma.

'Just act natural,' you mutter under your breath.

'This is me actin' natural,' Jayne protests in what he probably thinks is a whisper.

The line moves forward with mechanical precision. Each miner steps up, gets scanned by a handheld device that beeps green or red, then shuffles through. Simple enough. But as you watch, you notice the scanner's taking longer readings on some folks—and when it does, the guards ask questions.

You're three people away from the front now. The scanner in the guard's hand looks military-grade, the kind that might pick up more than just mining tools. Your pistol feels suddenly heavy under your coat, and you catch Zoe's subtle tension as she shifts her weight.

Two people ahead. One. Your turn.

The guard looks up at you with bored efficiency, scanner raised.

A.text: Smile confidently and step forward like you belong here
A.consequence: Confidence may work but scanner still detects anomalies, requiring quick thinking to explain
A.narration_directive: Mal's confidence gets him through initial interaction but scanner readings raise guard suspicion

B.text: Create a distraction by 'accidentally' bumping into Jayne
B.consequence: Distraction draws more attention and guards become suspicious of the entire group
B.narration_directive: The 'accident' backfires spectacularly, causing guards to focus on all three crew members

C.text: Try to charm the guard with small talk about mining conditions
C.consequence: Small talk distracts guard enough that scanner reading is cursory and misses weapons
C.narration_directive: Friendly conversation about mining conditions puts guard at ease and scan is perfunctory

D.text: Signal Zoe to be ready for trouble while you submit to the scan
D.consequence: Preparation helps but scanner still detects weapons, leading to tense standoff
D.narration_directive: Zoe is ready for trouble when scanner alerts, creating opportunity for coordinated response
